questions.question_text:
You want to augment a text classification dataset. You consider back-translation and LLM paraphrasing.

Which approach is MOST appropriate to ensure label preservation and avoid harming evaluation integrity?
options: A. Apply paraphrasing uniformly to train and test so metrics stay comparable
B. Only augment the minority class with aggressive paraphrasing without any checks
C. Augment training only, and add automatic label-preservation checks (e.g., semantic similarity thresholds, entailment/classifier agreement) plus a small human audit; keep original eval untouched
D. Replace all training data with paraphrases to increase diversity
questions.explanation: Augmentation should not contaminate evaluation. Paraphrasing/back-translation can change meaning, sentiment, or entity mentions, breaking labels. Practical pipelines augment training only, apply conservative filters (similarity/entailment, label-consistency checks), and audit samples. Keeping evaluation data original preserves comparability and avoids leakage.

questions.question_text:
You are evaluating an LLM that generates customer-support answers. Automated metrics (BLEU/ROUGE) don’t correlate well with user satisfaction.

Design a robust evaluation plan that includes: (1) a human evaluation rubric, (2) calibration/abstention thresholds (when the model should say “I don’t know” or escalate), and (3) how you’d reconcile offline evaluation with online outcomes. Mention at least one common pitfall.
questions.correct_answer:
Plan:
1) Human rubric: define dimensions aligned to product goals (e.g., factual correctness grounded in policy/docs, completeness, safety/compliance, tone, and actionability). Use clear scoring anchors (1–5) and pairwise preference where useful. Include adversarial and edge-case sets (policy exceptions, ambiguous requests).
2) Calibration/abstention: require citations/grounding for factual claims; abstain/escalate when retrieval confidence is low, when the answer would require personal data access, or when the model’s self-consistency/uncertainty is high. Implement thresholds using signals like retrieval score margin, entailment/verification checks, or a calibrated classifier predicting “answerable”.
3) Offline vs online: build an offline test suite representative of traffic (by intent/language/segment) and track rubric scores over time; then validate changes with A/B tests measuring user satisfaction, resolution rate, escalation rate, and safety incidents. Use post-deployment audits and disagreement analysis between offline rubric and online metrics.
Pitfalls: rubric ambiguity leading to low inter-annotator agreement; evaluating on leaked/templated data; over-optimizing to annotator preferences; ignoring latency/cost; not separating answerability from answer quality.
questions.explanation: Generative evaluation is a text-processing and product-measurement problem: you need grounded rubrics, calibrated abstention to avoid confident hallucinations, and a feedback loop connecting offline benchmarks to online behavior. Common failures include vague rubrics, unrepresentative test sets, and missing calibration signals that determine when to answer vs defer.

questions.question_text:
You’re improving search/RAG quality. You consider adding query rewriting (expansion) and spelling correction.

Which statement best captures the practical tradeoffs and how you should evaluate the change?
options: A. Query expansion always improves recall without hurting precision, so offline evaluation is enough
B. Spelling correction should aggressively correct to maximize lexical match, even if it changes intent
C. Rewriting/expansion can improve recall but may harm precision or change intent; evaluate with retrieval metrics (recall@k, nDCG), intent-sensitive slices, and online A/B tests with guardrails
D. Query rewriting is only useful for sparse (BM25) retrieval; it never helps dense retrieval
questions.explanation: Rewriting/expansion and spelling correction are text-processing changes that can shift intent. They often trade precision for recall, and can create harmful rewrites (e.g., correcting a brand name to a common word). Evaluate end-to-end: retrieval metrics, downstream answer quality, slice analyses (entities, brand terms, multilingual), and online testing with guardrails (CTR, satisfaction, deflection, latency). They can help both sparse and dense retrieval depending on the failure mode.

questions.question_text:
In production, you observe a steady increase in OOV/rare terms (new product names, slang, misspellings). Compare the impact and mitigations for:
1) a classical bag-of-words model (e.g., TF-IDF + linear classifier)
2) a subword-based embedding model (e.g., BPE/WordPiece transformer)

Provide at least 2 mitigations per case and how you’d monitor the issue.
questions.correct_answer:
1) TF-IDF/BoW: OOV terms are dropped entirely, losing signal and potentially shifting feature distributions; spelling variants fragment counts.
Mitigations: (a) use character n-grams or hashing vectorizer to capture unseen strings; (b) periodic vocab refresh/retrain with recent data; (c) add spelling normalization or synonym mapping; (d) backoff features (e.g., domain lexicons). Monitoring: OOV rate against fixed vocab, fraction of tokens mapped to UNK/ignored, drift in top features, performance by cohort (new products).

2) Subword transformer: fewer true OOVs because words decompose into subwords, but rare/new terms may split into many pieces, harming representation; distribution shift can still degrade.
Mitigations: (a) domain-adaptive continued pretraining/fine-tuning on recent corpora; (b) add retrieval or lexicon features for entities (product catalog grounding); (c) consider tokenizer augmentation/retraining if domain shift is large (with careful versioning); (d) spelling correction or normalization upstream (balanced against meaning loss). Monitoring: average subword pieces per word, rate of long subword sequences, embedding drift, performance on a “new terms” eval set, and cohort metrics by recency.
questions.explanation: Classical models are brittle to vocabulary shift because unseen terms vanish; character-level features and retraining help. Subword models handle novelty better but can still suffer when segmentation becomes inefficient or semantics are missing; adaptation, grounding, and careful tokenizer management mitigate. Monitoring must be tied to measurable proxies (OOV, pieces-per-word) and cohort performance.

questions.question_text:
You maintain a text preprocessing pipeline used by multiple models (classification, retrieval, and an LLM reranker). A recent tokenizer upgrade changed outputs and caused a regression.

Which set of practices best ensures end-to-end consistency and safe evolution of the text pipeline?
options: A. Allow each model team to implement their own preprocessing for flexibility
B. Freeze preprocessing forever; never change normalization/tokenization
C. Version the preprocessing + tokenizer as an artifact, define explicit input/output contracts, and add golden/regression tests that diff normalized text and token IDs across versions
D. Only monitor final model metrics; preprocessing details don’t need testing
questions.explanation: Text transforms are part of the model contract. Versioning preprocessing/tokenizers, documenting contracts (what normalization happens where), and maintaining golden tests (including tricky Unicode/emoji cases) allow safe upgrades and quick rollback. Relying only on end metrics makes debugging slow and can miss silent behavior shifts.

questions.question_text:
You ingest text from multiple sources (web pages, PDFs, user forms). You want automated gating to prevent low-quality text from entering training.

List 5 concrete ingestion-time quality checks you would implement, and for at least 2 of them describe a measurable threshold/heuristic you’d use to auto-reject or route to manual review.
questions.correct_answer:
Example checks:
1) Encoding/Unicode anomalies: detect high rate of replacement chars (\uFFFD), invalid UTF-8 sequences, or unusual control characters; threshold e.g., reject if >0.1% chars are \uFFFD or if control chars exceed a small count.
2) Language detection: run fast language ID; reject/route if confidence < X (e.g., <0.7) or if detected language not in allowed set.
3) Truncation: detect fields cut off (ends mid-token/sentence, abrupt end markers), or length exactly equals max field size; threshold e.g., if length == max_len and last char not sentence boundary, flag.
4) HTML/PDF extraction quality: measure text-to-markup ratio, boilerplate ratio, or OCR confidence; threshold e.g., reject if alphabetic character ratio < 0.3, or if average token length is extreme, or if repeated headers/footers dominate.
5) Dedup/boilerplate: detect near-duplicate blocks (headers/footers/legal disclaimers) and either strip or downweight; threshold e.g., if >60% of document matches known boilerplate shingles.

Other valid checks: profanity/PII detection, abnormal whitespace density, too many URLs, too many non-language symbols, perplexity/outlier scoring, broken sentence segmentation, missing required fields.
questions.explanation: Ingestion gating prevents expensive downstream failures and silent data poisoning. Effective checks are measurable and automatable: encoding integrity, language ID confidence, truncation signals, extraction quality proxies, and boilerplate/duplication detection. Thresholds should be tuned on a labeled sample and monitored for drift to avoid over-filtering.

questions.question_text:
You are implementing a chat-based LLM feature. User content is injected into a prompt template. You’ve seen failures where user text contains delimiters or strings like “### System:” that cause instruction bleed-through.

Which approach is MOST systematic for preventing prompt injection via formatting/escaping issues (as a text-processing problem)?
options: A. Concatenate strings with simple separators like "\n\n" and hope users don’t include them
B. Use a structured message format (role-tagged chat template), escape/encode user content, and enforce strict boundaries (e.g., JSON with schema validation) before rendering to the model
C. Add more examples in the prompt showing the model to ignore malicious inputs
D. Remove all punctuation from user input
questions.explanation: Treat prompt construction like a serialization problem: structured messages, strict delimiters, and escaping/encoding reduce ambiguity. Schema validation and canonical rendering prevent user-controlled content from being interpreted as instructions. Purely heuristic separators or “more examples” are weaker and easier to bypass; removing punctuation harms meaning and doesn’t guarantee safety.

questions.question_text:
You suspect evaluation leakage because your train/test split was done by random row sampling. The dataset contains templated customer-support messages with small variations (names, dates) and shared boilerplate.

Which split strategy is MOST effective to reduce leakage from near-duplicates and templates?
options: A. Hash exact raw text and remove identical duplicates only
B. Cluster by similarity using normalized text + shingling/MinHash (or embedding similarity) and split by cluster/group so near-duplicates stay in the same split
C. Increase test size so leakage matters less
D. Shuffle with a different random seed
questions.explanation: Exact dedup catches only identical strings; templated or lightly edited messages still leak. Group-based splitting using similarity clustering (character/word shingles + MinHash/LSH, or embedding-based clustering) keeps near-duplicates and boilerplate variants together, preventing train→test contamination and overly optimistic metrics.

questions.question_text:
In extractive QA, you have (context, question, answer_text) and you need start/end character offsets for training. Your pipeline performs Unicode normalization (NFKC), lowercasing, and HTML entity decoding before tokenization.

Describe a practical strategy to compute reliable answer spans and validate them automatically. Include at least one failure mode you would test for (e.g., multiple occurrences, normalization changes length).
questions.correct_answer:
Strategy: compute spans on the *exact* context string that will be fed to the model (post-normalization/decoding). If the dataset provides answer_text only, locate it in the normalized context using a robust matcher: (1) normalize both context and answer_text with the same function; (2) find all occurrences; (3) disambiguate using additional metadata (e.g., original offsets if available, surrounding window match, or choose the occurrence that best matches raw context alignment); (4) store start/end on normalized context.

Validation: add an automated check in preprocessing that slices context[start:end] and asserts it equals the normalized answer_text; if not, mark the example as invalid for extraction (route to generative QA or drop). Test failure modes: answer appears multiple times; HTML decoding changes length (“&” -> “&”); NFKC merges characters (e.g., full-width forms); accent stripping or lowercasing changes match; truncation of context causes span to fall outside model window.
questions.explanation: Span supervision is only correct if offsets refer to the same string the model sees. Normalization and decoding can change string length and character identity, breaking naive offsets. A robust pipeline normalizes consistently, uses deterministic matching and disambiguation, and gates/flags examples that fail invariants (slice-check).

questions.question_text:
You are building NER training data. Annotators label entities by character offsets in the *raw* text. Your preprocessing step normalizes whitespace and strips accents before tokenization.

What is the MOST robust way to avoid span alignment bugs (offset drift) between annotations and model inputs?
options: A. Always annotate on the fully normalized text that will be tokenized, and store both the normalized text and a mapping back to raw text if needed
B. Keep annotating raw text but hope modern tokenizers handle offsets correctly
C. Convert character offsets to token indices after tokenization without storing any mapping
D. Randomly drop punctuation during training to make the model robust to offset errors
questions.explanation: Offset drift happens when the text used for annotation differs from the text used for tokenization/model input. The most robust approach is to make the annotation surface match the model surface (annotate normalized text), or to maintain an explicit reversible mapping from raw→normalized positions. Without a mapping, converting offsets post-hoc is brittle, especially with Unicode normalization, accent stripping, or whitespace collapsing.

questions.question_text: Give a concrete example where lowercasing, accent stripping, or emoji handling causes a train/inference mismatch that changes model behavior. Then describe two practical safeguards you would implement to prevent this class of bug (one in code/process, one in testing/monitoring).
questions.correct_answer:
Example: model trained on normalized text where accents are stripped and emojis removed, so “I love café ☕️” becomes “i love cafe”. In production, if accents/emoji are preserved, tokenization changes (e.g., “café” OOV or different subword splits; emoji becomes its own token) shifting embeddings and predictions.

Safeguards:
1) Code/process: enforce a single shared preprocessing library/artifact used by both training and serving (same versioned function/container), with an explicit pipeline contract (input/output schema and normalization steps).
2) Testing/monitoring: golden tests with a curated suite of tricky strings (accents, mixed scripts, ZWJ emojis, variation selectors) that assert byte-level and token-level outputs; plus production logging of raw→normalized→tokens for sampled traffic and drift alerts on token distribution / OOV rate / normalization diffs.
questions.explanation: The key is that seemingly minor text transforms can change token boundaries and vocabulary membership, which changes downstream features/embeddings. Preventing it requires: (a) eliminating divergence via shared, versioned preprocessing; and (b) catching regressions with targeted golden tests and runtime drift monitoring (e.g., spike in unknown tokens, unexpected Unicode categories, or changed normalization outputs).

questions.question_text:
You trained a text classifier with a preprocessing pipeline that lowercases and strips accents (e.g., “café” -> “cafe”). In production, you notice a sudden accuracy drop for queries containing accented characters and emojis.

Which investigation is MOST likely to quickly confirm a train-time vs inference-time normalization mismatch and localize the cause?
options: A. Compare the distribution of raw Unicode code points and normalized tokens between training logs and production logs, and run the same normalization function on a shared sample to diff outputs
B. Increase model capacity (more layers/parameters) and retrain on the same data
C. Tune the learning rate schedule and retrain to improve generalization
D. Add more dropout and early stopping to reduce overfitting
questions.explanation: Normalization mismatches are best detected by comparing *inputs at each pipeline stage* across train and inference. Logging and diffing raw text, normalized text, and token outputs (including Unicode code point distributions) quickly reveals whether production is applying different rules (e.g., not stripping accents, different emoji handling, different Unicode normalization form). Model tuning won’t fix an upstream contract mismatch.

questions.question_text: You’re starting an annotation project for a text classifier (e.g., policy violation detection). You have limited budget and expect label ambiguity. Propose an annotation strategy and label schema design that reduces noise and supports iteration.
questions.correct_answer: A practical strategy: (1) Start with a small pilot to refine guidelines and label taxonomy; include edge cases and create a decision tree. (2) Prefer a schema that separates orthogonal concepts (e.g., violation_type vs severity vs target) and includes an explicit "uncertain/needs-review" or "insufficient context" label to avoid forcing guesses. (3) Use double-annotation on a subset, measure inter-annotator agreement, and run adjudication to update guidelines. (4) Implement active learning or uncertainty sampling to prioritize informative examples, plus stratified sampling to cover rare classes. (5) Track label versions and guideline changes; keep a golden set for regression testing. (6) Include quality controls (attention checks, annotator feedback loops) and define how to handle multi-label cases (allow multi-label or define precedence rules).
questions.explanation: Most production text tasks fail due to ambiguous labels and inconsistent guidelines rather than model choice. A pilot + iterative guideline refinement reduces systematic noise. A well-designed schema captures uncertainty and separates dimensions so the model can learn more reliably. Agreement measurement, adjudication, and versioning make the dataset maintainable over time.

questions.question_text: You need to deduplicate customer records coming from multiple sources. Names and addresses are messy (typos, abbreviations). Which approach is the most practical for entity resolution/fuzzy matching at scale?
options: A. Compare every pair of records with Levenshtein distance and merge if distance < 3.
B. Use deterministic exact match on email only; ignore all other fields to avoid false merges.

C. Use blocking (e.g., by normalized zip/phone prefix/soundex) to limit candidate pairs, then score pairs with a learned or weighted similarity model (token/char similarity + field weights), and set thresholds with human review for ambiguous merges.
D. Use a single global cosine similarity on raw name strings with no normalization; merge top-1 neighbor for each record.
questions.explanation: Entity resolution is a precision-critical pipeline. Practical systems use blocking to reduce O(n^2) comparisons, normalize fields (case, punctuation, abbreviations), and score candidate pairs using multiple signals (name similarity, address overlap, phone/email, geography). Thresholds often include a gray zone routed to manual review to prevent catastrophic false merges. Pure Levenshtein on all pairs doesn’t scale; email-only misses many matches; naive nearest-neighbor merging causes error cascades.

questions.question_text: You ingest semi-structured documents (HTML/PDF exports) that contain headings, paragraphs, tables, and code blocks. Describe a robust approach to parse them into canonical text for retrieval/LLM context, including how you’d handle tables and section boundaries.
questions.correct_answer: A robust approach: (1) Parse into a structured intermediate representation (DOM for HTML; layout-aware blocks for PDF via an OCR/layout tool) rather than plain text scraping. (2) Preserve section hierarchy (H1/H2, numbered headings) and emit canonical text with explicit separators/metadata (e.g., "## Section" markers) to keep boundaries. (3) Handle tables by converting to a normalized format: either row-wise key:value text, Markdown tables, or JSON-like records depending on downstream use; include header associations and avoid losing cell context. (4) Detect and preserve code blocks with fencing and language hints; avoid aggressive normalization that changes code tokens. (5) Chunk using structure-aware rules (by section, then by size) and attach provenance (doc_id, section_path, page). (6) Validate with unit tests on representative docs and add heuristics for boilerplate removal (nav/footer) with allow/deny lists.
questions.explanation: Canonicalization is about preserving meaning and navigability. Structure-aware parsing improves retrieval (better chunking) and reduces hallucinations (clear provenance). Tables and code are common failure points: flattening them naively destroys relationships, while structured conversion keeps them usable for both search and LLM prompting.

questions.question_text: You have a multilingual product where users often code-switch (e.g., Spanish + English in one message). In production, what pipeline design is most robust and cost-effective?
options: A. Force-translate everything into English with a single MT model; disable language detection to reduce latency.
B. Route by language using document-level lang-id only; if it says Spanish, use Spanish models and ignore embedded English terms.

C. Use script/token-level signals (or mixed-language detection), choose multilingual models or dual-encoding embeddings, preserve named entities, and optionally translate only when needed (e.g., for monolingual downstream components) with caching and fallbacks.
D. Reject any code-switched input as unsupported to keep the system simple.
questions.explanation: Code-switching breaks document-level assumptions. A robust production approach uses multilingual encoders (or separate encoders with shared space), detects mixed language, preserves entities/brand terms, and applies translation selectively (only for components that require it). Caching and fallbacks keep costs down. Forcing full translation can distort entities and add latency/cost; simplistic routing misprocesses mixed inputs; rejecting inputs harms UX.

questions.question_text: For evaluating a generative QA or summarization model, which evaluation plan is most appropriate beyond classic IR metrics like precision/recall?
options: A. Use only ROUGE because it fully captures factuality and helpfulness.

B. Use a mix: task-specific automatic metrics (e.g., ROUGE/BERTScore), factuality checks (citation/attribution, QA-based or entailment-based), human evaluation with a rubric (faithfulness, completeness, style), and targeted slice tests for hallucinations and refusals.
C. Use BLEU only; it correlates best with human judgment for all generation tasks.
D. Use retrieval metrics (nDCG/MAP) only; generation quality is irrelevant if retrieval is good.
questions.explanation: Generation quality needs multiple lenses: overlap metrics capture some fluency/coverage but miss factuality and usefulness. Practical evaluation includes faithfulness/hallucination checks (e.g., entailment, QA-based verification, citation correctness), human rubric-based review, and slice-based tests (long inputs, multilingual, ambiguous questions, safety). This reflects how production teams catch regressions that IR metrics cannot.

questions.question_text: You’re monitoring a text classification service in production and suspect text drift. Name 4 practical drift signals you would track and explain how each might indicate a problem.
questions.correct_answer: Examples of practical drift signals: (1) Token/subword distribution shift (e.g., KL divergence or PSI on top tokens) indicating new vocabulary, product launches, or spam; (2) Input length distribution shift (mean/percentiles) indicating truncation risk, new UI changes, or copy-paste behavior; (3) Language/locale distribution shift (lang-id proportions, script detection) indicating expansion to new markets or bot traffic; (4) OOV/unknown-token rate or character-level anomaly rate indicating typos, encoding issues, or new domains; optionally (5) embedding centroid drift, (6) topic cluster proportion changes, (7) label distribution shift / prediction entropy changes, and (8) increase in safety/PII flags. Each should be tied to alerts and a playbook (e.g., retrain, adjust preprocessing, add language routing).
questions.explanation: Text drift is rarely a single metric. Combining surface-form signals (tokens, length, language) with model-output signals (confidence/entropy, label proportions) helps distinguish genuine domain shift from transient noise or instrumentation changes. The goal is actionable monitoring tied to remediation steps.

questions.question_text: You need to add text logging/observability for a production LLM-based assistant. The system must be debuggable but privacy-safe. What is the best approach?
options: A. Log raw prompts/responses for all users to maximize reproducibility; redact later if needed.
B. Log only aggregate metrics (latency, token counts) and never log any text, even for debugging incidents.

C. Use tiered logging: default to minimal metadata + hashed identifiers; sample and store redacted text with PII detection, access controls, retention limits, and an incident-only escalation path; also log model/version and retrieval context IDs.
D. Replace all text with embeddings and store them forever since embeddings cannot leak sensitive info.
questions.explanation: Practical observability balances debugging with privacy: log structured metadata (model/version, prompt template ID, retrieval doc IDs, latency, token counts, safety flags), and only store text when necessary via sampling or incident workflows. When storing text, apply PII detection/redaction, strict access controls, encryption, and short retention. Embeddings can still leak information and shouldn’t be treated as risk-free. Logging everything raw is usually noncompliant and risky; logging nothing makes incident response and quality iteration too slow.

questions.question_text: You’re considering adding spelling correction before feeding user queries into a search + intent model. Which engineering tradeoff is most important to evaluate first?
options: A. Whether correction increases recall but harms precision by changing named entities/brand terms, and how to gate corrections using confidence/whitelists.
B. Whether spelling correction can be done with a larger GPU to reduce training time.
C. Whether correction always improves downstream accuracy regardless of language and domain.
D. Whether correction should be applied after ranking because it never affects retrieval.
questions.explanation: Spell correction can help recall (fixing misspellings) but can also introduce damaging edits (e.g., brand names, SKUs, person names) that reduce precision and user trust. Practical systems gate corrections by confidence, preserve protected tokens, use domain dictionaries, and sometimes run both original+corrected forms. The key is measuring downstream impact (retrieval metrics, intent accuracy, and user-facing errors), not just intrinsic correction accuracy.

questions.question_text: You’re building retrieval + classification for extremely short texts (search queries, product titles, SMS). You have sparse signals and lots of typos. Describe a practical modeling and feature strategy (at least 3 components) to improve robustness without overengineering.
questions.correct_answer: A good practical strategy combines: (1) character/subword-aware representations (BPE/WordPiece, char-CNN, or models robust to misspellings) to reduce OOV and handle typos; (2) query/title expansion or enrichment using lightweight signals (synonym dictionaries, learned expansion terms, or retrieving top-k similar items and using their text as context) to combat sparsity; (3) hybrid retrieval: lexical (BM25) + dense embeddings with a reranker, since short text often benefits from exact-match signals plus semantics; optionally (4) incorporate metadata (category, language, locale, time) and (5) calibrate thresholds / abstain for low-confidence cases. Keep it simple: measure gains via offline eval and A/B tests, and ensure latency constraints are met.
questions.explanation: Short text has limited context, so relying on only dense semantics or only lexical matching is brittle. Subword/character modeling improves robustness to typos and rare terms; hybrid retrieval captures both exact and semantic matches; expansion/enrichment adds missing context. Metadata and calibration are common production levers that often beat complex modeling changes.

questions.question_text: You’re training a text classifier for customer support ticket routing. You have ~20k labeled examples and see some class imbalance. Which data augmentation approach is the best *minimal, practical* choice to try first, and when should you avoid augmentation altogether?
options: A. Use back-translation or LLM-based paraphrasing only for minority classes, with strict semantic checks; avoid augmentation when labels depend on exact wording or compliance/legal phrasing.
B. Randomly shuffle words and randomly delete 30% of tokens; avoid augmentation only when you have too little data.
C. Replace every noun with a synonym from WordNet; avoid augmentation only when training a transformer model.
D. Always augment all classes equally with heavy paraphrasing to prevent bias; never avoid augmentation because more data is always better.
questions.explanation: For an AI/ML engineer, the most practical first augmentation is controlled paraphrasing/back-translation targeted at minority classes to reduce imbalance while preserving label semantics. You still need guardrails: semantic similarity thresholds, banned-term preservation, and spot checks. Avoid augmentation when the label hinges on exact phrasing (e.g., policy/compliance, contract clauses, medical instructions), when small wording changes flip the label, or when augmentation introduces label noise that outweighs benefits. Naive token shuffling/deletion and blanket synonym replacement commonly break meaning and degrade performance.

questions.question_text:
You’re ingesting semi-structured text for RAG: HTML pages, Markdown docs, and email threads. You want clean chunks with preserved code blocks and tables, and you must avoid indexing boilerplate (nav bars, footers) and quoted email history.

Describe an end-to-end extraction/cleaning strategy. Include at least: boilerplate removal, handling code blocks/tables, and email quote/thread parsing.
questions.correct_answer:
HTML: parse with a real HTML parser (not regex). Extract main content using readability/boilerplate removal (e.g., Readability, trafilatura) and drop nav/footer/ads via DOM heuristics (density, tag patterns, repeated blocks). Preserve structure by converting to a normalized intermediate format (e.g., Markdown/AST) with explicit nodes for headings, paragraphs, lists, code blocks, and tables.

Markdown: parse to an AST; keep fenced code blocks intact (don’t wrap/rehyphenate), and represent tables as structured rows/cells or a stable textual serialization. Keep heading hierarchy to drive section-based chunking.

Emails: detect and remove quoted history using common markers (lines starting with '>', 'On ... wrote:', Gmail/Outlook separators), strip signatures, and keep only the latest message (or store thread messages separately with metadata). Normalize line wraps without destroying paragraphs.

Indexing: chunk by semantic units (sections, list items, table rows) with metadata (source URL, heading path, email message-id, timestamp). Store both cleaned text and pointers to original raw content for citation. Add deduplication and a boilerplate fingerprint to avoid indexing repeated templates across pages.
questions.explanation: RAG quality depends on clean, structure-preserving extraction. The key is to use parsers and intermediate representations (DOM/AST) to avoid losing code/table boundaries, while removing boilerplate and quoted history that otherwise pollute retrieval. Maintaining metadata and raw pointers enables faithful citations and debugging.

questions.question_text:
You receive user inputs that may be English, Spanish, Arabic, or mixed (e.g., English product names inside Arabic sentences). You must normalize and tokenize for both retrieval (BM25) and embedding generation.

What is the best practical plan?
questions.correct_answer: Run language identification at the document and/or segment level (fastText/langid/CLD3), but handle mixed-language by allowing multiple labels or doing script-based segmentation (Latin vs Arabic vs CJK). Apply Unicode normalization (NFC/NFKC as appropriate) and language-aware casefolding; avoid naive lowercasing that breaks languages with special casing rules. For retrieval, use analyzers per language/script (Arabic normalization, stemming optional; Spanish accent handling; tokenization that respects script) and consider character n-grams for robustness to misspellings and code-switching. For embeddings, choose a multilingual encoder and keep normalization consistent with its training (don’t over-normalize away meaning). Store both raw and normalized forms and log detected language to drive downstream routing and evaluation.
questions.explanation: Multilingual pipelines fail when they assume one tokenizer/normalizer fits all. A strong approach combines language/script detection, per-language analyzers for lexical retrieval, and multilingual embeddings for semantic matching, while being careful about normalization choices that can change meaning or harm tokenization.

questions.question_text:
You’re building a text classifier (spam vs not spam) for short messages. You try two baselines:
1) Logistic regression on TF-IDF (word + character n-grams)
2) A small transformer fine-tuned on the dataset

In which scenario is the TF-IDF + linear model most likely to *win* (or tie) in practice, and why?
options: A. When you have limited labeled data, strong lexical cues, and need fast training/inference with easy debugging.
B. When you have abundant labeled data and need deep semantic understanding across long documents.
C. When the task requires reasoning over multiple paragraphs and coreference resolution.
D. When the input is multilingual with heavy code-switching and you cannot do language identification.
questions.explanation: Sparse lexical features with linear models are strong for problems dominated by surface patterns (URLs, obfuscations, keywords) and small-to-medium data regimes. They train quickly, are robust, and offer interpretability via feature weights. Transformers tend to shine with more data and tasks requiring compositional semantics and longer-range context (B/C). Multilingual code-switching (D) is not automatically better for either, but TF-IDF without language-aware preprocessing can degrade due to vocabulary fragmentation.

questions.question_text:
You need robust sentence segmentation and section parsing for messy inputs: PDFs converted to text (random newlines), emails (quoted replies), and documents with bullets/headers.

Which approach is most reliable as a baseline before adding ML?
options: A. Split sentences on every period followed by a space.
B. Use a rule-based + statistical segmenter (e.g., Punkt/spaCy) augmented with domain rules (bullet/header detection, email quote stripping), and validate with unit tests on representative samples.
C. Assume the LLM will infer sentences; skip segmentation entirely.
D. Split on newline characters only; PDFs always preserve sentence boundaries.
questions.explanation: Real-world text has abbreviations, decimals, ellipses, bullets, and layout artifacts. A proven segmenter plus targeted heuristics (handling “>” quotes, signature blocks, bullet prefixes, header casing/numbering) is a strong baseline. You also need tests because small rule changes can have large downstream effects. Naive splitting (A/D) fails frequently; skipping segmentation (C) makes downstream chunking, retrieval, and span alignment brittle.

questions.question_text:
You’re curating a large text corpus to fine-tune a model for customer-support QA. You also have a held-out evaluation set of real tickets.

List concrete steps you’d take to (1) sample/balance the training data, (2) decontaminate against the eval set, and (3) handle licensing/PII constraints. Be specific about techniques, not just principles.
questions.correct_answer:
1) Sampling/balancing: define target distribution (issue types, languages, time ranges, customer tiers). Use stratified sampling by label/topic and time to avoid temporal leakage. Downsample overrepresented boilerplate/auto-replies; upsample rare but important categories; cap per-customer or per-thread to reduce duplication. Deduplicate near-identical tickets (MinHash/SimHash on normalized text) and filter low-signal content (empty, signatures-only).

2) Decontamination: normalize both train and eval (casefold, whitespace, Unicode) and run exact dedup (hash) plus near-dup detection (n-gram Jaccard/MinHash, embedding similarity with threshold) to remove train examples that match or heavily overlap eval tickets. Also check for template overlap (same macros) and for paraphrased leakage via semantic similarity. Log removed items and keep a reproducible decontamination report.

3) Licensing/PII: track document-level provenance and license metadata; exclude incompatible licenses from training; maintain allow/deny lists. Run PII detection/redaction (emails, phone numbers, addresses, account IDs) with rules + ML, and store redaction maps if spans are needed. Ensure data retention policies, access controls, and audit trails. For evaluation, ensure the eval set is not used in training features (including retrieval indexes) to avoid indirect leakage.
questions.explanation: Model quality depends heavily on corpus construction. Practical answers mention stratification, dedup/near-dup, leakage checks beyond exact matching, and operational controls (provenance, reproducibility, and compliance). Decontamination must consider both lexical and semantic overlap, and licensing/PII constraints must be enforced via metadata and filtering, not ad-hoc manual steps.

questions.question_text:
In a modern NLP pipeline using subword tokenizers and embeddings, when does stemming/lemmatization still help, and when can it hurt?

Pick the best answer.
options: A. It always helps because it reduces vocabulary size and improves generalization for all models.
B. It is mostly obsolete; it never helps and should be removed from all pipelines.

C. It can help for sparse lexical baselines (TF-IDF/BM25) and some domain-specific matching, but can hurt semantics (e.g., conflating meanings), break exact-span tasks, and reduce interpretability/alignment with subword tokenization.
D. It only helps for languages that use Latin scripts; it never helps for other scripts.
questions.explanation: Stemming/lemmatization can improve recall for lexical retrieval and linear models by collapsing inflections (especially in morphologically rich languages), but it can also conflate distinct terms ("policy" vs "police" under aggressive stemming), harm tasks needing exact forms (NER spans, citations), and interact poorly with subword tokenizers where the model already handles morphology. The right choice depends on task, model class, and evaluation.

questions.question_text:
You must summarize a 2,000-page document corpus and answer questions with citations. Context windows are limited, and naive chunking loses global structure.

Which end-to-end workflow is most appropriate to maintain faithfulness and enable evidence-backed answers?
options: A. Concatenate all pages, send to the LLM, and rely on the model to ignore irrelevant parts.
B. Chunk into fixed windows, summarize each chunk once, discard the original text, and answer questions only from the summaries.

C. Build a hierarchical pipeline: segment by document structure, create chunk embeddings + metadata, retrieve candidate evidence for a question, perform map-reduce summarization over retrieved evidence, and generate answers with citations grounded in selected spans.
D. Randomly sample chunks until the token limit is reached, then ask the LLM to answer using that sample.
questions.explanation: Long-context workflows need *structure-aware segmentation*, *retrieval/evidence selection*, and *multi-stage aggregation*. Hierarchical summarization reduces information loss, retrieval keeps the prompt focused, and citations require maintaining links to original spans. Discarding originals (B) prevents later verification and can amplify summarization errors. A and D are unreliable and typically exceed context limits or miss key evidence.

questions.question_text:
System design: You’re building a people/company search feature. Users type queries like “Jon Smyth” and expect “John Smith” and “J. Smith” to match. The same normalized names are also used as features for a downstream ML model that predicts whether two profiles are the same person.

How would you design normalization and matching differently for (a) search retrieval and (b) ML entity resolution? Call out at least 3 differences and why.
questions.correct_answer:
Search retrieval: optimize recall with controlled normalization and fuzzy matching. Use query-time and index-time normalization (casefolding, Unicode normalization, accent folding), tokenization, and approximate matching (edit distance, n-gram/phonetic keys, synonyms/initial expansion). Keep multiple indexed fields (raw, normalized, phonetic, edge n-grams) and use ranking to resolve ambiguity.

ML entity resolution: optimize calibrated decisions and avoid lossy normalization that removes discriminative signal. Keep raw fields plus derived canonical forms as separate features. Use canonicalization for blocking (to reduce candidate pairs) but preserve evidence for the model (e.g., exact string, normalized string, token overlap, Jaro-Winkler, nickname match, address similarity). Track provenance and avoid over-normalizing (e.g., stripping middle initials may increase false positives). Ensure deterministic, versioned normalization so training/inference match.

Key differences: (1) Search uses aggressive fuzzy matching for recall; ER uses cautious canonicalization + rich features to manage precision. (2) Search can accept multiple candidate results; ER must output a binary/score decision with thresholds and needs well-calibrated probabilities. (3) Search normalization is often lossy and ranking can fix mistakes; ER needs to preserve raw signal and handle edge cases explicitly to avoid irreversible collisions (e.g., “Li” vs “Lí”).
questions.explanation: Search and ER share primitives (normalization, similarity) but differ in objective functions. Search is a retrieval/ranking problem; ER is a decision problem with asymmetric costs. A strong answer separates blocking/candidate generation from scoring, and emphasizes versioned, deterministic preprocessing for ML parity.

questions.question_text:
You have a dataset with entity span labels (start_char, end_char) on raw text. You need to normalize text (Unicode NFKC, lowercasing, whitespace collapsing) before tokenization. How would you preserve correct alignment between spans and tokens end-to-end?

Describe a practical approach and common pitfalls.
questions.correct_answer:
Use a reversible mapping between raw and normalized text and carry offsets through the pipeline. Practically: (1) normalize while building a char-level alignment map from normalized indices back to raw indices (or vice versa) by iterating through the raw string and emitting normalized characters with provenance; (2) convert raw labeled spans into normalized spans using that map (careful with many-to-one folds like ligatures, full-width forms, and whitespace collapsing); (3) tokenize the normalized text using a tokenizer that can return offset mappings (e.g., Hugging Face fast tokenizers) and map normalized char spans to token spans via offsets; (4) store both raw_text and normalized_text plus maps in your examples so debugging is possible; (5) add invariants/tests: the extracted substring from raw via mapped offsets should match the labeled entity after normalization.

Pitfalls: applying normalization after labeling without remapping offsets; collapsing whitespace/newlines changes indices; Unicode normalization can change string length (e.g., 'ﬁ' -> 'fi'); lowercasing can change length in some languages; PDF text extraction may insert spurious newlines; tokenizers may skip/strip characters causing offset gaps; and mixing byte offsets with char offsets (UTF-8) causes silent misalignment.
questions.explanation: Span supervision is only as good as your offset bookkeeping. Any transformation that changes string length or character boundaries must be accompanied by an explicit mapping. Fast tokenizers’ offset_mapping helps only *after* you decide which text version is canonical. Keeping both raw and normalized text (and a map) is a pragmatic engineering pattern that prevents label drift and makes errors diagnosable.

questions.question_text:
You’re building an LLM-powered information extraction endpoint that must return *valid JSON* with fields: {"invoice_id": string, "total": number, "currency": string}. The input text may contain quotes, braces, and code blocks. Which prompt/output strategy is the most robust for production parsing?

Assume you can change both the prompt template and the parser/validator.
options: A. Ask the model to "respond in JSON" and then run a regex to extract the first {...} block.
B. Wrap the source text in clear delimiters, instruct the model to output JSON only, parse with a strict JSON parser, and validate against a JSON Schema (retry with error feedback on failure).
C. Ask the model to output YAML because it is more human-readable, then convert YAML to JSON with a best-effort parser.
D. Ask the model to output a Python dict literal and use eval() to convert it to a dict.
questions.explanation: In production, you want *deterministic parsing and validation*. Clear delimitering reduces prompt injection and boundary confusion; strict JSON parsing avoids ambiguous formats; schema validation catches missing/wrong-typed fields early; and a controlled retry loop with validation errors is a standard robustness pattern. Regex extraction (A) is brittle (nested braces, multiple objects, hallucinated text). YAML (C) introduces additional parsing edge cases and security concerns. eval() (D) is unsafe and can execute arbitrary code.

questions.question_text:
You must extract invoice fields (invoice number, total amount, due date) from semi-structured text. You can choose:
- regex/rule-based extraction
- a supervised ML model
- an LLM extractor

How would you decide which to use, considering precision/recall targets, maintenance, and adversarial/malformed inputs? Give a concrete decision rule.
questions.correct_answer:
Decision approach:
- If the format is stable and you need very high precision with predictable behavior (e.g., finance workflows), start with rules/regex plus strict validation (checksums/patterns, currency formats) and fallbacks; rules are fast, cheap, and auditable.
- If formats vary across vendors and you have labeled data and strong latency/cost constraints, use supervised ML (sequence labeling or layout-aware models if you have coordinates) with validation post-processing.
- If formats are highly variable, labels are scarce, and you can tolerate some variability with human-in-the-loop review, use an LLM extractor—but wrap it with schema validation, delimiter/JSON escaping, length limits, and adversarial hardening.

Concrete rule: choose the simplest method that meets target precision at acceptable recall and operational cost; e.g., if regex+validators achieve ≥99.5% precision on a representative validation set, ship rules and route failures/low-confidence cases to ML/LLM fallback. Always add robustness controls (input limits, Unicode validation) and monitoring for drift/new templates.
questions.explanation: Extraction is an engineering tradeoff: rules excel at precision and predictability; ML generalizes with enough data; LLMs handle diversity but require guardrails and can be brittle to malformed inputs. A tiered system (rules → ML/LLM fallback) is common in production.

questions.question_text:
You need to segment messy documents into sentences/sections for downstream retrieval and summarization. Inputs include emails with quoted replies, bullet lists, headers, and text extracted from PDFs (line breaks in odd places). Multilingual punctuation may appear.

Which approach is MOST likely to work well in production?
options: A. Use a simple regex that splits on '.' and '\n' only; it’s fast and language-agnostic
B. Use a learned sentence segmenter trained on clean newswire; apply it directly to PDF text without preprocessing

C. Combine heuristics (quote stripping, bullet/heading detection, PDF line-join rules) with a robust segmenter (e.g., spaCy/Stanza/UDPipe), and evaluate on a labeled sample of your own formats and languages
D. Avoid segmentation; feed the entire document to the model and let attention handle structure
questions.explanation: Messy formats break naive rules and clean-domain segmenters. Production segmentation typically needs a hybrid: format-aware cleanup (emails/PDF artifacts) plus a segmenter that handles abbreviations and multilingual punctuation. Crucially, you must evaluate on in-domain labeled examples because segmentation errors cascade into retrieval and summarization quality.

questions.question_text:
You’re modeling noisy user-generated content (UGC) for toxicity detection. Examples include: "soooo BAD!!!", emojis, creative spelling, and inconsistent casing.

When can normalization HURT performance, and what is a practical compromise?
questions.correct_answer:
Normalization can hurt when it removes label-correlated signals: repeated characters ("soooo"), ALL CAPS, punctuation bursts ("!!!"), emojis, or stylized spellings that correlate with toxicity/intent. Over-normalizing can also erase demographic/identity terms’ distinctions or change meaning.

Practical compromise: keep raw text (or minimally normalized text) for modeling, but add controlled features/normalizers that are reversible or bounded (e.g., cap repeated chars to a max like 3, normalize Unicode to NFC, standardize whitespace) and rely on tokenizer/model robustness. Evaluate ablations: raw vs normalized vs mixed, and ensure the same preprocessing is used at train/inference.
questions.explanation: UGC often encodes intent in orthography. A balanced approach preserves predictive cues while preventing pathological inputs (e.g., extreme repetition) and keeping the pipeline consistent and robust.

questions.question_text:
You are designing a production text preprocessing pipeline used by multiple models (search indexing, classification, and analytics). You need to change normalization rules (e.g., new Unicode handling) without breaking downstream systems.

List THREE production-grade design requirements you would enforce (e.g., idempotency, determinism), and describe one regression test you’d add.
questions.correct_answer:
Three requirements (examples):
1) Deterministic and versioned preprocessing: same input + same version => same output; store the version with artifacts/features.
2) Idempotency where possible: applying the normalizer twice yields the same result, reducing compounding changes.
3) Backward compatibility / migration plan: ability to reprocess historical data or support multiple versions during rollout; clear deprecation policy.
(Other valid: explicit locale/language handling, strict input validation, reproducible tokenization settings, observability/metrics.)

Regression test example: a golden test suite of representative inputs (multilingual, emojis, messy whitespace, confusables) with expected normalized outputs + expected token offsets; run in CI and compare diffs when rules change. Additionally, run an offline A/B on a fixed evaluation set to check metric deltas and distribution shifts (e.g., token length histograms).
questions.explanation: Text preprocessing changes can silently alter features, offsets, and retrieval keys. Versioning, determinism, and idempotency make behavior predictable and debuggable. Golden tests catch unintended changes early, while metric/regression checks ensure model and search quality don’t degrade.

questions.question_text:
You’re training a classifier on forum posts. Each “thread” has multiple posts, and the label is at the thread level. Many users post in multiple threads, and you also have timestamps.

Which split strategy best reduces leakage while reflecting production performance?
options: A. Randomly split individual posts into train/val/test to maximize data in each split
B. Split by thread ID so posts from the same thread never cross splits; optionally also do time-based evaluation to simulate future threads
C. Split by label so each split has identical label distribution, even if threads are mixed
D. Split by taking the first 80% of posts in each thread for training and the last 20% for test
questions.explanation: Thread-level labels imply strong intra-thread similarity; mixing posts across splits leaks content and style. Splitting by thread prevents near-duplicate leakage. Adding time-based splits can further match production (train on past, test on future) and reveal drift. Random post-level splits and within-thread splits are classic leakage patterns.

questions.question_text:
You’re building an NER pipeline. Labels come as character offsets in the raw text, but you apply normalization (case-folding + Unicode NFC + collapsing multiple spaces) before tokenization.

What is the BEST practice to avoid span misalignment?
options: A. Normalize the text first and then reuse the original character offsets; offsets are stable under normalization
B. Tokenize first on the raw text and then normalize tokens individually; offsets can be reconstructed later without bookkeeping

C. Keep a mapping from raw character indices to normalized character indices (or avoid destructive normalization before labeling), and use tokenizer-provided offset mappings computed on the exact string used for tokenization
D. Convert labels from character offsets to word indices using split(' ') before any preprocessing
questions.explanation: Any normalization that changes string length/positions (space collapsing, Unicode normalization, removing punctuation) breaks raw offsets. The robust approach is: tokenize the exact string you will feed the model and obtain offset mappings from that tokenizer; if you must normalize, maintain a deterministic index mapping from raw→normalized (and ideally normalized→raw) so spans can be transformed correctly. Simple whitespace splitting fails on punctuation, multiple spaces, and multilingual text.

questions.question_text:
A public-facing text ingestion API feeds both analytics and an LLM-based assistant. You notice occasional crashes and strange outputs.

Which defense is MOST relevant to robust text processing against adversarial/malformed inputs (beyond prompt injection)?
options: A. Only add a prompt-injection classifier; malformed text is handled by the LLM
B. Enforce input limits (bytes, tokens, max token length), normalize/validate Unicode (e.g., NFC + confusable detection), and escape/validate delimiters when serializing to CSV/JSON
C. Convert all input to ASCII by dropping non-ASCII characters to remove attack surface
D. Base64-encode all text and store it without decoding to avoid parsing issues
questions.explanation: Real systems break on pathological inputs: extremely long tokens (quadratic tokenizer behavior), Unicode confusables (spoofing), invalid code points, and delimiter injection (CSV/JSON/SQL/log forging). Practical defenses include strict size limits, tokenizer-safe constraints, Unicode normalization/validation, confusable detection where relevant, and correct escaping/structured serialization. Dropping all non-ASCII harms multilingual usability; base64 just defers the problem and breaks downstream processing.

questions.question_text:
You have a 20-page document classification task (e.g., contract risk). The base encoder has a 512-token limit. You already tried naive chunking + majority vote and it fails when the key evidence is sparse.

Name TWO document-level aggregation strategies beyond simple chunking, and explain one common pitfall of sliding-window evaluation.
questions.correct_answer:
Two aggregation strategies (examples):
1) Hierarchical modeling: encode chunks, then aggregate with an attention layer/transformer over chunk embeddings (hierarchical pooling/attention) to let the model focus on salient chunks.
2) Evidence-style pooling: max/attention pooling over chunk-level logits, or a learned gating/mixture-of-experts that selects top-k chunks; alternatively, retrieval-then-read where a retriever selects relevant spans and the reader/classifier consumes only those.

Sliding-window pitfall: evaluation leakage/overcounting—if you label every window with the document label, many windows won’t contain evidence and become noisy negatives/positives; also, overlapping windows make metrics overly optimistic because near-duplicate windows appear in both train/val or inflate confidence. Another pitfall is thresholding at window-level rather than document-level, causing miscalibrated doc predictions.
questions.explanation: Long-document tasks often need explicit mechanisms to find sparse evidence and aggregate it. Hierarchical attention and top-k evidence pooling are common practical approaches. Sliding windows can distort both training signal (label noise) and evaluation (overlap-induced dependence), so you must define the prediction unit (document) and aggregate deterministically with careful split hygiene.

questions.question_text:
You are implementing text normalization for two systems:
1) a search engine for customer names and addresses
2) a text classification model for detecting policy violations

Which normalization choice is MOST appropriate?
options: A. Apply aggressive normalization (case-folding, accent folding, punctuation stripping) for both search and modeling to reduce vocabulary size

B. For search, use case-folding and accent folding but preserve some punctuation/token boundaries; for modeling, be cautious—preserve signals like casing/punctuation when they are predictive, and rely on the model/tokenizer for robustness
C. For search, avoid any normalization to keep precision high; for modeling, strip everything including emojis to avoid noise
D. Always normalize by removing all diacritics and punctuation because it cannot change meaning
questions.explanation: Search and modeling have different objectives. Search often benefits from normalization that increases recall (case-folding, accent folding) while still respecting token boundaries and certain punctuation (e.g., hyphens/apostrophes in names) to avoid precision loss. For modeling, aggressive normalization can remove predictive cues (ALL CAPS, repeated punctuation, emojis) that correlate with labels (e.g., harassment, urgency). Modern tokenizers/models can handle variation; you typically normalize only what’s necessary and keep the pipeline deterministic and consistent with training.

questions.question_text:
You maintain a support-ticket classifier with labels {"refund_request", "bug_report", "account_access"}. A teammate proposes augmenting data by generating synthetic examples that mention the label name in the text (e.g., "This is a refund_request ticket").

What is the PRIMARY risk, and what’s the best mitigation?
questions.correct_answer:
Primary risk: label leakage—models learn to key off the injected label tokens rather than the underlying semantics, inflating offline metrics and failing in production.

Mitigation: never include label names (or other training-only artifacts) in the input text; keep prompts/templates label-agnostic. If using synthetic generation, constrain prompts to produce realistic user text, run leakage checks (search for label tokens/regex), and evaluate on a clean, production-like holdout set (ideally time-based and/or source-separated).
questions.explanation: Synthetic generation can accidentally create shortcuts. If the label string appears in the input, the model can trivially predict it, yielding misleadingly high validation accuracy. Good mitigations include strict prompting, post-generation filters, and leakage-oriented audits (token/substring checks, nearest-neighbor overlap checks) plus realistic holdouts.

questions.question_text:
You’re building a sentiment classifier for product reviews. You have only 2k labeled examples and the model overfits. You consider adding paraphrases/back-translation to augment the training set.

Which approach is MOST likely to improve generalization while minimizing label drift?
options: A. Paraphrase every example with an LLM and keep the original label, no filtering needed because paraphrases preserve meaning by definition

B. Use back-translation/paraphrasing only for examples where the label is based on global sentiment, then filter augmented candidates with a consistency check (e.g., teacher model agreement or sentiment lexicon constraints)
C. Prefer synonym replacement at random for all tokens because it cannot change labels
D. Augment only the test set so evaluation includes more linguistic variety
questions.explanation: Paraphrasing/back-translation can help when the label is tied to overall semantics (e.g., sentiment) but it can still cause label drift (negation changes, intensity changes, sarcasm introduced/removed). In practice, you reduce drift by (1) applying augmentation selectively (avoid tasks where labels depend on exact phrasing, entities, or compliance language), and (2) filtering/validating augmented samples via agreement checks (teacher model, heuristics, or round-trip constraints). Random synonym replacement can easily break grammar/meaning and can change sentiment; augmenting the test set is incorrect because it contaminates evaluation design.

questions.question_text:
You’re formatting user-provided text into an LLM prompt. You need strong separation between system instructions and untrusted user content, and you want to reduce prompt-injection risk from user text that contains strings like "Ignore previous instructions".

Which approach is best?
options: A. Concatenate strings directly: `prompt = system + user_text`
B. Put user text in quotes and hope the model respects it

C. Use structured message roles (system/user), wrap untrusted text in clear delimiters, escape/encode delimiter collisions, and keep a minimal, fixed system prompt; optionally apply allowlist-based templating for tool calls
D. Remove all punctuation from user text before sending it to the LLM
questions.explanation: Prompting is text processing: you want reliable boundaries and fewer injection surfaces. Using role-separated messages (where supported), explicit delimiters, and escaping prevents user content from breaking the template. Minimal fixed system prompts reduce ambiguity. For tool/function calling, templating and schema validation further constrain behavior. Simply concatenating or quoting is brittle; removing punctuation destroys meaning and doesn’t reliably prevent injection.

questions.question_text: You ingest text from PDFs, web pages, and CSVs. You see mojibake like “FranÃ§ais”, mixed encodings, and weird artifacts like hyphenation at line breaks ("inter-\nnational"). Which strategy is best for a production pipeline?
options: A. Assume UTF-8 everywhere; if decoding fails, drop the document
B. Use `errors='ignore'` always so the pipeline never crashes

C. Detect/standardize encoding (with fallback), normalize Unicode, preserve raw bytes or original text for traceability, and add targeted cleanup for common extraction artifacts (hyphenation, broken ligatures) with monitoring
D. Convert everything to ASCII to avoid encoding issues
questions.explanation: Production ingestion needs robustness and observability. Encoding detection/fallback (or source-specific decoding), Unicode normalization, and retaining raw/original text enable debugging and reprocessing. Targeted cleanup for known artifacts (PDF hyphenation, ligatures like “ﬁ”, spurious newlines) improves quality without overly destructive transformations. Blindly ignoring errors (B) can silently delete characters and change meaning; dropping documents (A) hurts coverage; ASCII conversion (D) loses information and harms multilingual content.

questions.question_text: You maintain a production text preprocessing + tokenization pipeline used both for training and inference. Which practice best enforces consistency and prevents silent regressions?
options: A. Document the pipeline in a wiki and rely on code review
B. Version the preprocessor/tokenizer as an explicit artifact, add feature-parity tests (golden inputs -> expected outputs), and run backward-compatibility checks before rollout
C. Only pin the Python package versions in `requirements.txt`
D. Re-train the model whenever preprocessing changes, without tests
questions.explanation: Treat preprocessing/tokenization as a contract: versioned artifacts (including normalization rules, special tokens, and vocab) plus golden tests catch changes like altered Unicode normalization, regex tweaks, or different tokenization libraries. Pinning dependencies helps but doesn’t prove behavioral parity. Retraining without tests can still fail if inference uses different preprocessing or if changes break downstream assumptions (e.g., max length, feature indices).

questions.question_text:
You’re building sentence/paragraph segmentation for downstream summarization. Input includes:
- English and Spanish
- Bullet lists and headers
- Email threads with quoted replies (lines starting with ">")
- Abbreviations like “Dr.” and “U.S.”

Describe a robust approach (not just a single regex). What structure would you preserve for the model?
questions.correct_answer:
A good approach:
- Use a proven sentence segmenter (e.g., spaCy or Stanza) with language-specific models instead of naive splitting on periods.
- Preprocess document structure first: detect and separate quoted blocks (`^>+`), signatures, and headers; treat bullet items as separate units; preserve blank lines as paragraph boundaries.
- Keep a hierarchical representation: document -> sections/headers -> paragraphs -> sentences -> spans with offsets into the original text.
- Preserve offsets and markers (e.g., `is_quote`, `is_bullet`, `header_level`) so downstream summarization can weight or exclude quoted history and avoid mixing list items into sentences.

Optionally: add locale-aware rules for abbreviations, handle ellipses, and normalize newline conventions before segmentation.
questions.explanation: Reliable segmentation is rarely solved by a single regex because punctuation is ambiguous and varies by locale. For summarization, preserving structure (paragraph boundaries, list items, quoted blocks) often matters as much as sentence boundaries. Offsets enable traceability and debugging (e.g., highlighting source sentences) and help enforce invariants when text is re-tokenized later.

questions.question_text: You need to extract order IDs from customer emails. IDs look like “ORD-123456” but emails also contain tracking numbers, phone numbers, and quoted threads. When should you prefer a rule-based regex solution over training an ML model?
questions.correct_answer:
A strong answer:
- Prefer regex/rules when the pattern is stable, well-specified, and high precision is required (e.g., billing/fulfillment); when labeled data is scarce; and when you need predictable failure modes and easy auditing.
- Add context rules to control false positives (e.g., require prefix `ORD-`, length constraints, ignore quoted history blocks, ignore signatures, validate against checksum/database).
- Prefer ML when patterns are fuzzy/variable, context-dependent, or you need recall across many formats/vendors; or when maintenance cost of rules explodes.

Example regex (with boundaries): `r"\bORD-[0-9]{6}\b"` plus pre-cleaning to remove quoted threads and normalizing whitespace.
questions.explanation: This tests the ML-vs-rules boundary. In many production systems, a small set of well-designed rules beats an ML model on precision, latency, and maintainability—especially when the identifier format is contractual. However, rules can become brittle if the surface forms vary widely; then ML (or hybrid) becomes attractive. Interviewers want to hear about precision control, validation, and how you reduce false positives in messy email text.

questions.question_text: A bag-of-words sentiment model predicts positive for: “The camera is not good.” Why does this happen, and what is a simple mitigation that often works well for linear models?
options: A. The model is overfitting; mitigation is to add more L2 regularization only
B. Bag-of-words ignores word order, so “not” and “good” are treated independently; mitigation is to add bigrams or explicit negation features (e.g., ‘NOT_good’)
C. Tokenization is wrong; mitigation is to remove stopwords so “not” is dropped
D. The model needs stemming; mitigation is to stem “good” to “goo”
questions.explanation: Unigram bag-of-words loses compositionality: the strong positive weight for “good” can dominate. Adding bigrams ("not good") or engineered negation handling (mark words following a negation cue within a window) allows the linear model to represent the interaction without needing a deep model.

questions.question_text: Your dataset is multilingual (English, Turkish, and German). You’re considering applying the same English stemmer to all text before training a linear classifier. What is the best guidance?
options: A. Do it: stemming is language-agnostic because it only shortens words
B. Avoid it: stemming/lemmatization should be language-specific; otherwise you may destroy morphology and introduce noise, especially in morphologically rich languages like Turkish
C. It’s fine as long as you apply Unicode normalization first
D. Prefer stemming only for German because compounds are long
questions.explanation: Stemming/lemmatization depends heavily on language morphology. Applying an English stemmer to Turkish can severely corrupt tokens, fragmenting rather than consolidating features. For multilingual pipelines, either detect language and apply language-specific normalization, or use approaches less sensitive to morphology (character n-grams, subword tokenization, or multilingual models). Unicode normalization helps consistency but doesn’t solve morphological correctness.

questions.question_text: For a TF-IDF + logistic regression baseline on English product reviews, when is stemming/lemmatization most likely to help, and when can it hurt?
options: A. It always helps because it reduces vocabulary size without changing meaning

B. It helps when morphological variants dilute counts (e.g., “connect/connected/connecting”), but can hurt when conflating distinct meanings or harming interpretability (e.g., “policy” vs “police” stemming errors)
C. It hurts only for neural models, not for TF-IDF models
D. It helps only if you also remove stopwords and punctuation
questions.explanation: Stemming/lemmatization can improve recall by merging inflections, which can help linear models with sparse features. But aggressive stemming can conflate different words, distort named entities, and reduce interpretability. Lemmatization is usually safer but requires language-aware tools and may be slower. The impact is dataset-dependent and should be validated with ablations.

questions.question_text: You observe that your retrieval chunker uses “~800 tokens per chunk” but users report missing context. You discover the chunker estimates tokens by splitting on whitespace, while the model uses a BPE tokenizer. What are two practical debugging steps and one mitigation you would implement?
questions.correct_answer:
Good answer should include:

Debugging steps (examples):
1) Measure the distribution of (whitespace_tokens, model_tokens) on real corpora and compute worst-case ratios by language/script and by presence of URLs/emojis/code.
2) Log and compare the exact text boundaries: for a sample of chunks, record the raw chunk text, the model tokenizer token IDs, and the resulting token count; verify whether truncation happens and where.

Mitigation (examples):
- Use the same tokenizer as the model for chunk sizing (or a calibrated estimator with safety margin, e.g., target 650 model tokens for an 800 budget), and add an overlap strategy to reduce boundary loss.

Optional extras: unit tests with golden examples; alerting when chunk token counts exceed budget; language-aware chunk sizing.
questions.explanation: Whitespace splitting is a poor proxy for BPE/Unigram tokenization, especially for URLs, punctuation-heavy text, CJK, emojis, and mixed scripts. Debugging should quantify mismatch and identify where truncation removes relevant spans. Mitigation is aligning the chunker to the model tokenizer (or using conservative margins + overlap) so retrieval and generation are operating under the same token budget assumptions.

questions.question_text: You trained a transformer model using Tokenizer A. In production, someone swapped in Tokenizer B (different vocab/normalization). The model’s max length is 2048 tokens. Which outcome is the most likely and why?
options: A. No issue: tokenizers are interchangeable as long as they output integer IDs
B. The model will run but accuracy may degrade because the ID-to-subword mapping and special tokens differ, and truncation boundaries will shift
C. The model will crash immediately because PyTorch requires the same tokenizer object at inference
D. Accuracy will improve because a different tokenizer increases vocabulary coverage
questions.explanation: Transformers learn embeddings for specific token IDs; changing the tokenizer changes how text maps to IDs (including normalization rules, byte handling, and special tokens). Even if shapes match, semantics won’t. Additionally, token counts can differ, causing different truncation and chunking, which changes what context the model sees. Frameworks won’t necessarily crash; they’ll happily feed different IDs into the same embedding table—silently wrong.

questions.question_text:
Write a Python function that counts the top-K tokens from a very large newline-delimited text file (one document per line) without loading the whole file into memory.

Constraints:
- Must stream the file
- Must handle mixed newline conventions
- Should be reasonably robust to weird Unicode characters

Return: list of (token, count) pairs.
questions.correct_answer:
Example solution:

```python
from collections import Counter
import re
import unicodedata

TOKEN_RE = re.compile(r"\b\w+\b", re.UNICODE)

def normalize_text(s: str) -> str:
    # NFKC reduces compatibility variants; casefold is stronger than lower()
    s = unicodedata.normalize("NFKC", s)
    return s.casefold()

def top_k_tokens(path: str, k: int = 50, encoding: str = "utf-8"):
    counts = Counter()
    # newline=None enables universal newlines (\n, \r\n, \r)
    with open(path, "r", encoding=encoding, errors="replace", newline=None) as f:
        for line in f:
            line = normalize_text(line)
            # simple word tokens; could swap to a tokenizer as needed
            for tok in TOKEN_RE.findall(line):
                counts[tok] += 1
    return counts.most_common(k)
```

Notes: use streaming iteration over the file handle; `errors="replace"` prevents crashes on bad bytes; `newline=None` handles mixed newlines; `unicodedata.normalize` + `casefold` improves consistency.
questions.explanation: Interviewers look for streaming (iterate over the file handle), not `read()`/`readlines()`. Robustness: `newline=None` (universal newlines) and `errors='replace'` (or `'ignore'`) prevent pipeline failures from encoding glitches. Normalizing with NFKC and `casefold()` reduces vocabulary fragmentation. A `Counter` is a standard approach; for extreme scale you might shard, use Count-Min Sketch, or map-reduce, but the core requirement is memory-safe streaming.

questions.question_text:
You’re building a text normalization function for a product-review classifier (TF-IDF + linear model). Which pipeline is generally the safest default to reduce noise without destroying meaning?

Assume English-heavy but may contain emojis, URLs, and user handles.
options: A. Lowercase + Unicode NFKC normalization + normalize whitespace + replace URLs/emails/handles with placeholders + keep punctuation (or selectively strip) + preserve negations
B. Lowercase + remove all punctuation + remove all stopwords + stem everything aggressively + drop any token containing digits
C. Convert to ASCII (strip accents) + remove non-letters + remove short tokens (<4 chars) + collapse repeated characters to 1
D. Only lowercase; everything else should be learned by the model to avoid bias
questions.explanation: A is a pragmatic default: NFKC fixes many Unicode variants (full-width forms, compatibility characters), whitespace normalization prevents spurious token splits, and placeholdering URLs/handles reduces sparsity while keeping the signal that a URL/mention exists. Over-aggressive stripping (B/C) can remove sentiment cues (e.g., “not”, “!”, emojis), numeric info, and named entities. Doing almost nothing (D) often increases vocabulary noise and hurts baselines.

questions.question_text: You’re shipping a multilingual text pipeline (classification + search) to 15 locales. Inputs can be mixed-language and mixed-script. Which production approach is most likely to reduce failures and make evaluation meaningful?
options: A. Force-translate everything to English using an MT system; evaluate only on English metrics to simplify
B. Run language ID (including mixed-language detection), apply locale-aware normalization/tokenization, and maintain per-locale evaluation slices and thresholds
C. Use the same tokenizer and normalization for all languages; differences are negligible with modern models
D. Drop any input that contains multiple scripts to avoid edge cases
